text: This is Dr. Ben Taylor, cardiothoracic surgeon at St. Alphonsus in Boise. I'll be showing you a video today of a left upper lobectomy done with the da Vinci robot system.
You're gonna see why, if you have lung cancer, you're gonna wanna have your surgery here with us at St. Alphonsus in Boise. We are the only hospital system in Idaho performing robotic lobectomies.
The video will show you why this is the state of the art way to treat lung cancer surgically.

Lung cancer surgery used to be done in an open fashion with a very large incision and what we would consider today to be fairly crude instruments. Now the robot allows the surgeon to have three arms, advanced energy for dissection. It allows us to operate in very small spaces with very small incisions.
Basically to remove a lobe of the lung, you have to divide all the arteries to that lobe, all the veins to that lobe, and the airways to that lobe. The robot is perfect for this application.

The surgeon uses a stapler device with robotic instruments, demonstrating their range of movement and dexterity to perform precise surgical maneuvers. The heart is visible inside the pericardium on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide the pulmonary vein to the upper lobe, effectively sealing and cutting the vessel.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to safely seal and divide the bronchus, which is the airway to the upper lobe.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide the bronchus, typically the final step in the lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon places the removed specimen into a specialized retrieval bag, marking the completion of the operation.
